coredata.dc:title: Lignin fractionation-inspired carbon dots to enable trimodule fluorescent sensing of pH, silver ion and cysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Pengfei
Xu, Jikun
Hou, Xinyan
Dai, Lin
Xiao, Xiao
Zhang, Chuntao
Huo, Kaifu
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) inspire an emerging subclass of nanocarbons that is the topic of worldwide interest to smart materials with versatile functions. Despite an ideally sustainable biopolymer of industrial lignin with congenital phenylpropanoid skeleton, interior linkages and functional groups (e.g., –OCH3, –OH and –COOH), efficient lignin valorization is controversial by its heterogeneous structure. Herein, a “library” of CDs from technical lignin with gradient structure and narrow molecular dispersity is fabricated initiating by stepwise solvent fractionation, and lignin-derived CDs that function as the nanoprobe are thoroughly characterized with the interplay between fluorescence responses and sensing properties. Benefitting from the direction of molecular weights, β-aryl ether, hydroxyl/carboxyl groups and particle size by refined lignin, their bimodal emissive CDs radiate the switchable and controllable blue-green color under a wide excitation span, which can be modulated to trimodule detect pH, silver ion and cysteine with an acceptable selectivity and sensitivity in a broad concentration scale. It thus is believed that this work not only delivers a facile strategy to overcome the lignin heterogeneity, but also paves the avenue to rationally nanoscale-design multifunctional lignin valorization with a perspective of broadening the biorefinery frontiers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin fractionation
Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensing
Biorefinery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of lignin-derived fluorescent carbon dots
Impact of solvent fractionation on the lignin structures
Photoluminescence sensing performance of lignin carbon dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32171728 and 31700511 ), and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2018T110770 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of industrial lignin. Basic structural linkages of lignin including (a) side-chain δC/δH 50–90/2.5–6.0 and (b) aromatic regions δC/δH 100–135/5.5–7.5 in the HSQC NMR spectrum; (c) main substructures of lignin identified in the 2D HSQC NMR spectra including β-aryl ether (A, β-O-4′ linkages with a free –OH at the γ-carbon), Cα-oxidized β-O-4′ substructures (A′), resinol (B, formed by β–β′, α-O-γ′, and γ-O-α′ linkages), phenylcoumaran (C, formed by β-5′ and α-O-4′ linkages), syringyl (S), oxidized syringyl (S′), guaiacyl, (G), free p-coumaric acid (pCA), and p-hydroxyphenyl units (H); (g) quantitative functional groups of hydroxyl and carboxyl in lignin. CS: condensed syringyl unit. CG: condensed guaiacyl unit. Total P-OH: the total content of phenolic hydroxyl; (d) quantitative 31P NMR spectrum of lignin and (e) the corresponding content of functional groups (mmol/g); (f) the molecular weight distribution of lignin.

Schematic description (a) of the sequential lignin refining approach via solvent fractionation using ethyl acetate, ethanol, and acetone, hydrothermal processing of carbon dots from well-fractionated lignin, and fluorescent sensing of silver ion, pH and cysteine; the molecular weight distributions of (b) L1, (c) L2, (d) L3, and (e) L4; the recovery yield (f), quantitative functional groups (g), and zeta potential (h) of lignin fractions. L1: ethyl acetate dissolved lignin; L2: ethanol dissolved lignin; L3: acetone dissolved lignin; L4: residual lignin.

The particle size distributions (a-d), SEM images (e-h), and FTIR spectra (i-l) of lignin, corresponding to L1, L2, L3, and L4 respectively. L1: ethyl acetate dissolved lignin; L2: ethanol dissolved lignin; L3: acetone dissolved lignin; L4: residual lignin.

Characterizations of lignin carbon dots (LCDs) from L1 (CDs-1), L2 (CDs-2), L3 (CDs-3), and L4 (CDs-4). The nanoparticle size distributions (a-d), zeta potential (e), UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectra (f-i), and PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths (j-m) of LCDs, respectively.

PL mapping image (a-d) of lignin carbon dots (LCDs); the fluorescence responses (e-h) of LCDs with the existence of various pH value (pH 1–14), and the corresponding fitting curves (i-l) between fluorescence variation with pH value. From left to right, CDs-1: carbon dots from ethyl acetate dissolved lignin (L1); CDs-2: carbon dots from ethanol dissolved lignin (L2); CDs-3: carbon dots from acetone dissolved lignin (L3); CDs-4: carbon dots from residual lignin (L4).

The fluorescence responses (a-d) of lignin carbon dots (LCDs) with the existence of various metal ions (2 mM); the PL emission spectra (e-h) of LCDs quenched by different Ag+ concentrations (0–4000 μM); the linear fitting plots (i-l) of the (F 0 −F)/F 0 value with the varied concentrations of Ag+ (F and F 0 refer to the FL intensity with and without metal ions, respectively); the overlay fluorescence spectra (m-p) of the LCDs/Ag+ solution upon addition of various concentrations of L -cysteine. From left to right, CDs-1: carbon dots from ethyl acetate dissolved lignin (L1); CDs-2: carbon dots from ethanol dissolved lignin (L2); CDs-3: carbon dots from acetone dissolved lignin (L3); CDs-4: carbon dots from residual lignin (L4).

head.ce:title: Lignin fractionation-inspired carbon dots to enable trimodule fluorescent sensing of pH, silver ion and cysteine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) inspire an emerging subclass of nanocarbons that is the topic of worldwide interest to smart materials with versatile functions. Despite an ideally sustainable biopolymer of industrial lignin with congenital phenylpropanoid skeleton, interior linkages and functional groups (e.g., –OCH3, –OH and –COOH), efficient lignin valorization is controversial by its heterogeneous structure. Herein, a “library” of CDs from technical lignin with gradient structure and narrow molecular dispersity is fabricated initiating by stepwise solvent fractionation, and lignin-derived CDs that function as the nanoprobe are thoroughly characterized with the interplay between fluorescence responses and sensing properties. Benefitting from the direction of molecular weights, β-aryl ether, hydroxyl/carboxyl groups and particle size by refined lignin, their bimodal emissive CDs radiate the switchable and controllable blue-green color under a wide excitation span, which can be modulated to trimodule detect pH, silver ion and cysteine with an acceptable selectivity and sensitivity in a broad concentration scale. It thus is believed that this work not only delivers a facile strategy to overcome the lignin heterogeneity, but also paves the avenue to rationally nanoscale-design multifunctional lignin valorization with a perspective of broadening the biorefinery frontiers.

ce:section.ce:para: With the endeavor of carbon neutrality, the exploration of technologies to upgrade biomass into valuable fuels, chemicals and materials is a topic of great importance for the sustainable development of humankind (Liao et al., 2020; Robertson et al., 2017; Shuai et al., 2016; Xiao et al., 2021). As a representatively nonedible biomass, lignocellulose mainly comprises of three fundamental elements (i.e., lignin, cellulose, and hemicelluloses). Present biorefining strategies are focused on the efficient valorization of lignocellulose into cellulosic biofuels, such as ethanol, gasoline and diesel, while lignin conversion is still elusive (Qu et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2022). From the worldwide paper industry, an average annual byproduct of lignin that is crudely burned to generate heat outputs around 50–70 million tons (Dou et al., 2021). It must be emphasized that canonical lignin as a heterogeneous aromatic biopolymer holds the huge potential to deliver a renewable precursor for the chemical and material industries (Chen et al., 2022; Lizundia et al., 2021). In order to foster the industrial feasibility of biorefinery, especially to boost the value of lignin stream, various paradigms aiming at waste-free lignocellulose valorization have been explored in the last few years (Pang et al., 2021a).

Due to the high diversity in composition, structure and properties, carbon-based products from lignocellulose endow a burgeoning future in a wide variety of industrial territories (Fang et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). As a rising branch of carbon household, carbon dots (CDs) are recognized as a novel 0D form that displays the discrete and quasi-spherical nanoparticles with average diameter of < 10 nm, which consisted of carbon skeleton and affluent functional groups with specific quantum confinement, edge effects, and structural defects. Responding to their estimable optoelectronic traits, the utility of CDs is widely accepted in diverse territories including biosensor, energy storage, catalysis and anti-counterfeiting (Ðorđević et al., 2022; He et al., 2022). Note that, the well-designed approach of fabricating tunable emissive CDs is of utmost importance to foster the technical advances of carbon-based luminescent materials. With the perspective of trash-to-treasure, renewable lignin can be efficiently harnessed to prepare CDs with its virtues of eco-friendly and worldwide distribution. More importantly, it is confident forecasting that natural lignin with high carbon content appears irresistible to synthesize CDs because of its graphite-like phenylpropanoid structure and abundance functional groups, which offers a synergistic occasion of assembling the carbonaceous framework (e.g., sp 2/sp 3 hybridization) and surface decoration (e.g., intrinsic and edge defects) of CDs (Xu et al., 2021b). For instance, a phosphorous acid-aided hydrothermal procedure was developed for producing lignin-derived graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that can be adopted to construct a heterojunction with g-C3N4, thereby enabling an excellent photocatalytic decomposition capability towards methylene blue and lignin model compound over 93% (Liu et al., 2021). Taking advantage of a convenient tandem approach, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from lignin macromolecules that functioned as fluorescent nanoprobe exhibited a strongly sensitive response to Fe3+ ions with the concentration of 0–300 μM (Zhu et al., 2021a). With the aid of N, S-contained acid additives, the bright fluorescent lignin-CDs emitted tunable blue-green colors, which can be applied as ink production for anti-counterfeiting printing (Zhu et al., 2021b).

It is widely proposed that the inherent heterogeneity (e.g., molecular weight distribution, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) not only causes undesirable properties of lignin valorization (Pang et al., 2021b), but also blocks the interplay between its specific structure and CDs properties. Stepwise lignin fractionation, which is an effective tactic to screen lignin structure with narrow divergences, serves as a facile and tunable upgrading procedure to seize well-designed fractions displaying homogeneously chemical and physical characters (Cao et al., 2021; Pylypchuk et al., 2021). Herein, we innovatively integrate the solvent-fractionation technique with lignin-CDs preparation to offset the gap between uniform lignin fractions with photoluminescence CDs. The molecular properties of fractionated lignin streams as well as the optical and trimodule sensing properties of the as-prepared fluorescent CDs are comprehensively analyzed using gel permeation chromatography (GPC), multimode in-situ nuclear magnetic resonance (2D-HSQC and 31P NMR), photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and fluorescent sensing experiments. It thus is expected to maneuver the performance of lignin-CDs with a navigation from the controllable and refined molecular properties of lignin fractions.

Industrial lignin was kindly supplied from Shandong Longlive Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Jinan City, China). In the present work, the brief procedures of manufacturing alkaline lignin are as follows: the hydrothermal treatment of agricultural corncob was first conducted to eliminate hemicelluloses; after cascade-step residue extraction with dilute alkaline solution including aqueous sodium hydroxide, the separated effluent was further adjusted to acidic condition (~ pH=2) for precipitating the alkaline lignin, and then oven-dried at 60 °C for 48 h. Prior to use, crude lignin was rinsed with acidic water (pH=2) to eliminate impurities and then oven-dried in a vacuum cabinet at 60 °C for 24 h. All chemical reagents were of analytic grade, such as ethyl acetate, ethanol, acetone and tetrahydrofuran. Ultrapure water was applied throughout the whole experiment with a stable resistivity of approximately 18 MΩ·cm.

The original industrial lignin was subjected to a stepwise solvent-fractionation procedure to recover its four fractions. Briefly, industrial lignin and the first solvent of the sequence were homogeneously mixed into a round bottom flask equipped with magnetic stirring. With a fixed lignin to solvent ratio of 1:20 g mL−1, the resulting suspension was continually stirred for 6 h under ambient environment. Afterward, the insoluble lignin was isolated from the mixture using a paper filter while the filtrate was concentrated under reduced pressure. The aforementioned insoluble lignin fraction was dissolved in the subsequent solvent with a guidance of conducting the similar extraction approach as before. The solvents chosen for the sequential fractionation were ethyl acetate (L1), ethanol (L2) and acetone (L3) respectively. Finally, the residue insoluble lignin in the mentioned successive solvents was separated by filtration and denoted as L4. Prior to use, we freeze-dried all of the extracted-lignin fractions that generally presented a thin maple powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of lignin-derived fluorescent carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of lignin carbon dots (LCDs) was carried out using a facilely hydrothermal procedure. During a typical process, well-fractionated lignin (L1–L4) of 1 g and phosphoric acid of 5 mL were uniformly dissolved in 100 mL ultrapure water at the same time. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was heated hydrothermally in a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. The corresponding LCDs products were harvested via a facile membrane filtration, and then dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off 500 Da) to eliminate unreacted lignin oligomers. Ultimately, we adopted a range of advanced instruments to illustrate the physicochemical properties of lignin as well as the fluorescent sensing performances of LCDs (Supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of solvent fractionation on the lignin structures
ce:section.ce:para: The principal objective of this research is to explore how the molecular weight and multi-scale functionality of detailed lignin fractions dominate the physical dimensions and chemical properties of their derivative products. Consequently, we have combined GPC and NMR techniques to evaluate the macromolecular structure of pristine lignin, which is of great concern to build the bridge between lignin heterogeneity with solvent fractionation. The 2D-HSQC spectra and representative subunits of lignin with the related assignments of 13C–1H cross signals are demonstrated in Fig. 1a-b, c and Table S1, respectively. The precise recognition of classical substructures and interior linkages from natural lignin, including methoxyls (–OCH3), β-O-4′ aryl ethers (substructure A), Cα-oxidized β-O-4′ linkage substructures (substructure A′), phenylcoumaran (substructure B, β–5′), and resinol (substructure C, β–β′) linkages could be accomplished on account of our previous work (Xu et al., 2021a). It thus is believed that the technical lignin mainly interlinks with high abundance β-O-4′ ether (substructure A), accompanying by the presence of β5′ and ββ′ bonds. Another side, the obvious clues of syringyl (S), Cα-site-carbonyl-oxidized syringyl item (S′), guaiacyl (G), p-hydroxyphenyl (H) and p-coumaric acid (pCA) cross-signals can be readily located in the aromatic region (Xu et al., 2021a, 2021b).

In general, the molecular dimensions and physicochemical performances of lignin and its related products are highly depended upon the molecular weight distributions and interior functional groups (Cao et al., 2021; Gioia et al., 2018). In this vein, the detection of aliphatic hydroxyls, phenolic hydroxyls and carboxyls from lignin via the quantitative 31P NMR technique is carried out. We can easily locate the individual peaks of aliphatic OH (1.722 mmol g−1), phenolic OH (total P-OH, 2.6371 mmol g−1), and COOH (1.5974 mmol g−1) from Fig. 1d. The specific content of lignin-contained hydroxyls is summarized in Fig. 1e, which indicates that the largest species of phenolic OH appears to be guaiacyl OH (G-OH, 1.0126 mmol g−1) followed by p-hydroxyphenyl and syringyl OH (H-OH vs. S-OH, 0.6715 vs. 0.5198 mmol g−1), agreeing with the representative properties of a herbaceous lignin from biorefining industries. A small amount of condensed-OH contents is calculated to be 0.1733 mmol g−1 and 0.2599 mmol g−1 for CS-OH and CG-OH respectively, which potentially offers a testimony for the germination of lignin condensation with a finite degree. It also can be reinforced the point that the presence of oxygen-contained active spots (e.g., –OCH3, –OH and –COOH) in the original lignin might ignite the in-situ intercalation into nanomaterial, which is conducive to enhance the surface functionalization and defect engineering of LCDs. Moreover, the inherent phenylpropanoid structure grafted with aliphatic side-chain from lignin can be functioned as a significant regulator to tune the ratio of hybridized sp 2/sp 3 carbon skeleton. The weight average (M w 2975 g mol−1) and number-average (Mn 660 g mol−1) molecular weights of industrial lignin are depicted in Fig. 1f. The polydispersity index (PDI, M w/M n) of 4.1 indicates the wide divergence of raw lignin macromolecules that blocks their well-designed conversion into aromatics and materials. Therefore, we propose the reproducible solvent-fractionation approach not only to reduce the natural heterogeneity, but also to impel the versatile functionality of lignin from the molecular dimension.

A procedure sketch for the facile fabrication and trimodule fluorescent sensing of LCDs from well-fractionated lignin is demonstrated in Fig. 2a. It is clear that we can employ alkaline lignin as a cut-price precursor which also is easily available from biorefinering and pulping industries with a bright scale-up future. Considering the demands of waste-free biorefinery as well as the characteristic impedances of heterogeneous lignin, herein we rationally engineer a new type of LCDs with pH, silver ion (Ag+) and cysteine-sensitive performance, which can be facilely synthesized through hydrothermal processing of solvent-fractionated lignin. In this vein, the gradient divergence in chemical functionality and substructure linkages of natural lignin holds the promise to ignite a response behavior of LCDs (nano-size, carbon state sp 2/sp 3 hybridization, and surface functional groups) and the related fluorescent sensing properties. Moreover, the potential aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of lignin macromolecules that might physicochemically adsorbed on LCDs surfaces is a positive contributor to their luminescence (Ai et al., 2021). Consequently, it is highly desired to offset a vacant position in the direct correlation between lignin quantitative molecule-level structures with their deuterogenic nanomaterials.

The sequence of extraction is designed with the intention of retrieving lignin fractions that have the ability to exhibit the gradually increasing molecular weights and homogeneous structures (Gioia et al., 2018). The molecular weight distributions of fractionated lignin are illustrated in Fig. 2b-e. It is inescapably clear that the well-designed solvent system can consecutively fractionate the industrial alkaline lignin into four individual fractions following the rule of incremental molecular weight from 840 (L1) to 6850 g mol−1 (L4), which suggested that the smaller lignin molecules would be apt to dissolve in ethyl acetate and ethanol. Thus, the tandem system via adjusting lignin solubility in the chosen solvent is cost-effective to the controllable fractionation of technical lignin with ideal molecular weight. From the PDI listed in the Fig. 2b-e, it is easily found that the M w/M n value of fractionated lignin is also obey with the progressive order from 1.24 (L1) to 3.72 (L4), which are lower than that of the initial lignin. Benefitting from the narrow molecular weight distribution of dissociated lignin fractions, it thus is expected that their low polydispersity is quite beneficial for the ultimate application while lignin is adopted as the substitution to fabricate functional materials (Jin et al., 2022). Regarding the yields in Fig. 2f, the first two fractions (L1 and L2) account for approximately 62%, while L3 presents the moderate yield of 5%. A residue of L4 is extracted at the end of this process, possessing a yield of 15.25%. It might also be noted that the purification and filtration of fractionated lignin lead to an inevitable weight loss of lignin fractions. Besides, the lignin oligomers with lower molecular weights are difficult to precipitate via a facile acidic water procedure, resulting in a reduced yield of total lignin fractions that should also be taken into account. The polarity, solubility and sequence of each solvent might be of huge potentials to synergistically provoke the unique yield and detailed structure of lignin fractions.

To announce the potential interpretation for the controlled synthesis and optical performance of LCDs in a further step, it is highly indispensable to quantify the functional groups (e.g., –OH and –COOH) of individual lignin fractions via 31P NMR technique. As can be seen from Fig. 2g, different lignin fractions possess an assignable disparity in the contents of phenolic OH, aliphatic OH and COOH, which also proves that the stepwise solvent fractionation is a successful paradigm to tune the surface groups of lignin. In the fractionated lignin, the content of aliphatic hydroxyl group is gradually increased from 1.287 to 2.365 g mol−1, howbeit the content of phenolic OH (2.576–3.929 g mol−1) and COOH (1.517–2.846 g mol−1) reduced with the enlargement of molecular weights. The dominant species of all the lignin fractions is observed to be phenolic OH, implying that the industrial lignin used in this research have experienced a common decomposition pathway. It is universally known that the lignin dissociation from macromolecules to oligomers strongly depends on the cleavage of β-O-4′ linkages, thereby liberating free phenolic OH that might be formed at the C4 position. In this context, a higher reaction activity and hydrophilicity can be achieved by the additional phenolic OH in a lower molecular weight lignin, which is conducive to the preparation and functionality of lignin-based material (Mahmood et al., 2016). It has been witnessed that the more hydrophilic lignin fraction tends to self-assemble nanoparticles with higher zeta potential (Liu et al., 2019). As presented in Fig. 2h, the zeta potential of these lignin fractions are less than − 20 mV, suggesting exterior charge uniformity and stability of lignin samples.

It is well known that the chain entanglement dominates polymer aggregation via hydrogen bond, π-π interaction and electrostatic attraction (Zhao et al., 2017). In this work, the particle size of lignin is strongly affected by its molecular weight and structural heterogeneity ( Fig. 3a-d). With the increase of M w and PDI, the average nanoparticle sizes of lignin is gradually increased from 369.71 nm (L1, Fig. 3a) to 634.86 nm (L4, Fig. 3d). A more hydrophobic structure of lignin generally depends on its higher molecular weight, which presages that lignin polymers are readily prone to produce aggregates in the anti-solvent precipitation procedure of recovering their particles. The SEM images of lignin fractions are displayed in Fig. 3e-h, which is a proof of aforementioned relationship between lignin molecular weight and its particle size. The L1 fraction exhibits a well-isolated granule shape and arrangement (Fig. 3e), however, the lignin agglomeration generally formed with the incremental M w. To discriminate the functional groups grafted on biopolymers, it is generally appreciated that the Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is an impactful technique via the unraveling of vibrational modes. To be specific, some bands can be assigned with high confidence in the range of 1000–3000 cm–1 (Ma et al., 2020). As illustrated in Fig. 3i-l, all of lignin spectra exhibited a broad and intense band at 3450 cm−1, which is attributed to the O–H stretching in aliphatic structures. The absorption of C–H asymmetric and symmetrical vibrations from methylene (–CH2) and methyl (–CH3) groups is located at around 2900 cm–1, and the weak peak of 2800 cm−1 is corresponded to the methoxyl (–OCH3) groups. A stretching peak at approximately 1750 cm−1 can be contributed to the presence of conjugated ester and unconjugated carbonyl linkages derived from phenolic acids. In particular, the aromatic skeletal vibrations are explicitly confirmed by the bands at 1600, 1520 and 1430 cm−1, enabling a cardinal phenylpropanoid structure of lignin. The vibrations with aromatic skeletons and C–H deformations also corresponds to the absorbance at 1480 cm−1. The aromatic out-of-plane C–H bending easily provokes another absorbance at about 830 cm−1. According to a recent report, the presence of syringyl and guaiacyl structures in lignin molecules also can be located via their distinctive peaks in all the spectra (Ma et al., 2020). The FT-IR spectra present the analogous profiles among the lignin fractions with a few varieties about the relative intensity of specific absorbance, suggesting that the proposed solvent fractionation is a reversibly physical operating unit and offers a negligible alternation of basic lignin structure (Jin et al., 2022). These consequents offer the essential evolution to comprehend the engineering role of lignin fractions in the realization of highly efficient multi-color LCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence sensing performance of lignin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size of CDs functions as a guiding light to their quantum confinement and optical properties (Ai et al., 2021; Xia et al., 2019). DLS results presented in Fig. 4a-d reveal that the well-dispersed LCDs have the homologous average particle sizes of < 3 nm. Their average lateral sizes are about 2.81, 2.56, 2.32 and 2.28 nm for CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4, respectively. Through the initially stepwise solvent fractionation, the molecular weight and functional groups of lignin fractions would be the effective and facile way to trigger the reduced particle size of LCDs with the sequence of solvents. It is generally accepted that the PL emissive properties of CDs have experienced a blue-shift phenomenon with the reduction of average nanosize (He et al., 2022), which is accordance with the present work. The zeta potential results of CDs from fractionated lignin are depicted in Fig. 4e. CDs-1 has the highest zeta potential value (−13.5 mV) followed by CDs-2 (−12.7 mV) and CDs-3 (−12.1 mv), while the CDs-4 occupies a lower zeta potential of − 9.8 mV. We thus postulate that the above data conforms to the –COOH contents of their congeneric lignin fractions, affirming that the charge status of LCDs is highly correlated with the carboxyl in lignin. In brief, the lignin fractions in-situ import electron-withdrawing groups (e.g., –COOH and –OH) on the surface of LCDs, which are performed to not only effectively maneuver the degree of graphitization but also frame their diminishing particle size with blue-shift tunable emission.

As shown in Fig. 4f-i, the absorption spectra of the LCDs display identical excitonic absorption bands at approximately 270 nm, which is generally provoked by π-π * migration of the conjugated CO/CC links in sp 2 hybridized carbonaceous kernel and similar to the absorption characteristics of traditional CDs within the UV region (Li et al., 2019b; Xu et al., 2021b; Zhu et al., 2013). The inset picture of Fig. 4f-i demonstrates that the multicolor of LCDs solution is rapidly switched from yellowish under ordinary light to dual green-blue fluorescence with UV lamp of 400 nm wavelength. With the decrease of LCDs particle size from CDs-1 to CDs-4, the color of LCDs is gradually altered from green to blue. The normalized PL spectra of LCDs also exhibit reduced PL peaks at about 550–480 nm. Under the various excitation wavelengths with 10 nm interval ranging from 360 nm to 460 nm, the fluorescence emission spectra of LCDs are shown in Fig. 4j-m. Owing to the effect of quantum confinement, the LCDs presents an excitation-stimulated emission activity that is a familiar profile of CQDs due to their ordered graphitic structure (Kurian and Paul, 2021; Wang et al., 2020). From Fig. 4j-m, the PL emission spectra of LCDs ensure the continuous diversifications that exhibit dual fluorescence centers (near Em-1: 450 nm and Em-2: 550 nm). It is also clear that the bimodal blue-green emission of the LCDs derives from the excitation at 380 nm and 430 nm, respectively. As compared to the green-emissive peak, the fluorescent blue signal gradually strengthens with the increase of the molecular weights in the lignin precursor, and finally, leaves an intense bimodal emission distribution in CDs-4 that blue fluorescence outplays green fluorescence. Initiating by the π–π transition and the largely conjugated structure of ordinary CDs, the fluorescence of aqueous LCDs solution exhibits the luminescence of their carbonaceous nucleus (Xu et al., 2021c). Herein, the hybridization level of carbonaceous kernel (i.e., lignin aromatic ring decides sp 2 bond and aliphatic branching chain governs sp 3 structure) and its oxygen species (i.e., –OCH3, –OH and –COOH) might be the pivotal motive power for LCDs fluorescence via modulating the core-shell electronic environment. Meanwhile, the gradual increase of LCDs size from 2.28 to 2.81 nm concurs with the corresponding PL wavelength of switchable blue-green emission, illustrating the presence of quantum size effect (Wang et al., 2020). In Fig. 5a-d, three-dimensional (3D) visible PL mapping of LCDs also provide a direct information about the relationship between their excitation and emission wavelengths, in which two excitation peaks (near Ex-1: 380 nm and Ex-2: 430 nm) associated with the cross-linked PL emission band. Additionally, the alterable dual PL behavior of green-blue emission is clearly observed that could be adjusted by the molecular weights and functional groups of lignin fractions.

It has been claimed that the deprotonation of functional groups grafted on CDs at a elevated pH value would guide a brand-new surface state between their lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) (Kurniawan et al., 2022), thereby not only altering the bandgap but also shifting the maximum PL emission spectrum. Fig. 5e-h depicts the impact of wide pH values ranging from 1 to 14 on the maximum PL emission features of the four as-prepared LCDs. All of the LCDs display the similar fluorescent quenching behavior when the pH value is gradually increased. It also is important to emphasize that only the CDs-4 illuminates a red-shift behavior with the gradual increase of pH value (Fig. 5h), while other CDs manifest the weak blue-shift of PL emission at a alkaline region. The unique fluorescence sensitivity of LCDs under varied pH environment should be attributed to the protonation and/or deprotonation of hydroxyls on the surface of LCDs that directly inherits from lignin aliphatic and phenolic OH (Wang et al., 2022). Benefitting from the PL intensity ratio (F/F 0) varieties as a consequence of pH values, we evaluate the fluorescent pH sensing capacity by which F 0 and F define the PL intensity of LCDs in deionized water and diverse pH environments respectively. The typical non-linear curves need to be adopted ranging mostly from pH 2 to 14 to well-fitting the experimental data for LCDs except CDs-4 which authenticates a linear line starting from pH 2 to 10 (Fig. 5i-l). Among the LCDs from CDs-1 to CDs-3, the non-linear fitting profile of CDs-1 deliver a sensitive pH quenching performance with coefficient R 2 of 0.9457 (Fig. 5i), which could be assigned to the abundant hydroxyls groups of L1 as compared with the other lignin precursors. Benefitting from the quantitative equilibrium between aliphatic and phenolic OH, the CDs-4 situation might reach a compromise of deprotonation and protonation degree to yield the excellent pH sensing capacity with a linear coefficient R 2 of 0.9813 (Fig. 5l). It thus is plausible that the chemical environment of OH is not the sole parameter to trigger the pH sensitivity of LCDs. Instead, the combination of hydroxyl form, particle size and lignin molecular state could lead to a synergetic benefit of pH-manipulated band-gap tunability, delivering the origin for the pH amenability of LCDs.

Hazardous metal ions and organic pollutants contaminated wastewater represents an inevitable environmental issue of global concern. Biomass-related CDs have ignited burgeoning interests in the fluorescent detection of metals and organics using the “off-on” pathway, owing to their competitive virtues of simple manipulation, excellent sensitivity, and high-speed response (Zhang et al., 2021). As presented in Fig. 6a-d, the LCDs with a fixed dosage is adopted to forecast the selectivity towards diverse metal ions. Different metal ions covering Hg2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+ and Ag+ are added into the aqueous LCDs solution with a final concentration of 2 mM. Subsequently, we collect the PL spectra under a fixed excitation wavelength at 400 nm to examine the responsive ability. All of the LCDs enable that the FL intensity decreases remarkably with the adjunction of Ag+ whereas other metal ions exhibit weak influence on their FL intensity, which indicates that the as-prepared LCDs have an acceptable selectivity to recognize Ag+ ions. As illustrated from Fig. 6e-h, the fluorescence quenching titration is carried out by varying Ag+ solution over a broad concentration spanning up to 4000 μM, which demonstrated that the LCDs allows a typical behavior of dose-dependent response. We observe the universal phenomenon that a gradual reduction in the fluorescence intensity of LCDs solution is corresponded to the increase of Ag+ ion concentration, while the responding capacity is varied with different species of LCDs. As can be seen from Fig. 6i-l, the CDs-1 exhibits a fluorescent sensing performance of Ag+ ions in a concentration range from 100 to 1600 μM with a linear coefficient of R 2 0.9332. With the Ag+ concentration of 400–2200 μM, a relatively higher linear correlation (R 2 =0.9581) is fitted for the CDs-2. About the CDs-3 suspended in a wide concentration of Ag+ solution (100–2000 μM), a linear fitting curve is established via percent quenching (R 2 =0.9324). Among all of the LCDs, the CDs-4 endows a limited linear coefficient of R 2 0.9297 with the Ag+ concentration of 100–1600 μM. Note that, the color of LCDs solution looks continually faded as the concentration of Ag+ increases. It thus is emphasized that the fluorescent sensing range and linear relationship can be altered by selecting the task-specific CDs species, which potentially act as a convenient and intuitive naked-eye sensor for metal ions.

Towards fluorescent quenching of the LCDs, the selectivity and sensitivity for Ag+ ions are possibly due to the coupling actions, such as a stable affinity of Ag+ ions onto carbonaceous interface, larger radius of Ag+ ion and its ability to produce a non-fluorescent complex with the LCDs. It also is reasonable that Ag+ ion functions as a coordinating center to agglomerate LCDs via synchronously binding with the oxygen-containing functional groups from LCDs, thereby altering the electronic structure of LCDs to trigger the non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation (Huang et al., 2017). Therefore, it is conceivable to assume that the LCDs capable of Ag+ identification are partially attributed to the coupled mechanism of rapid chelating kinetic and stable attraction between nomadic Ag+ ions with deprotonation groups on the edge of CQDs, which can be controlled via varying the molecular weight, hydroxyl (i.e., aliphatic and phenolic OH) and carboxyl of fractionated lignin.

In the meantime, it is fortunate that the addition of cysteine could recover the fluorescence of LCDs initially quenched by Ag+. The influence of cysteine concentration (0–2000 μM) on the PL intensity of LCDs/Ag+ complex is plotted in Fig. 6m-p. Of note, the CDs-4/Ag+ system holds an acceptable ability as an “off–on” fluorescent probe to detect cysteine, probably owing their weak chelation compared with the other LCDs, which is conducive to form cysteine-Ag+ intermediates that easily escaped from the CDs-4 surface. Thus, the elimination of Ag+ ions bound on the LCDs surface ignites the restoration of unbound LCDs fluorescence (Borse et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2019a). Taking advantage of the changes in LCDs fluorescent intensity toward Ag+ and L -cysteine, which can be efficiently utilized as an “off–on” nanoprobe to apperceive the varied concentration of pollutants. Meanwhile, a visible color change is observed during the determination of Ag+ and L -cysteine. Last but not least, the concentration-dependent dual green-blue emissive fluorescent LCDs from well-fractionated lignin can be constructed for fluorescent trimodal detection of pH, silver ion and cysteine, which brings a possible scenario to impel the versatility of waste-free biorefineries with application-worthy products.

With the arrival of waste-free biorefinery, lignin-powered economies endow huge potential to alleviate the dual threats of technical availability and environmental pollution. An in-depth understanding of lignin complexity is indispensable to create sustainable value-chains from advanced lignin nanomaterials. In this work, we propose a sequential solvent fractionation to isolate industrial lignin with gradient molecular weights and functional groups (e.g., –OH and –COOH) confined in a narrow heterogeneity. A trimodule fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of pH, Ag+ and L -cysteine upon a broad scale via using dual blue-green emissive LCDs from well-fractionated lignin is reported. Through increasing lignin molecular weight, the nanoparticle size of LCDs is gradually decreased with a switchable PL behavior from green to blue emission. The number of –OCH3 and P-OH groups in the aromatic skeleton, as well as, the β-O-4′ linkages, aliphatic OH and COOH in branching chains from lignin fractions with the controllable heterogeneity also influence the structure and morphology of the LCDs to a large extent. As a guiding light for the structure-properties-performance interplay, a “library” of lignin fractionation-inspired LCDs with task-specific sensing ability is manipulated to enable the excellent fitting coefficient. The present procedure not only facilely settles inherent lignin heterogeneity but also broadens the practical lignin valorization in detailed nanomaterial production.

Pengfei Zhou: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jikun Xu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xinyan Hou: Writing – review & editing. Lin Dai: Writing – review & editing. Xiao Xiao: Writing – review & editing. Chuntao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Kaifu Huo: Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171728 and 31700511), and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018T110770).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2013

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, S., Meng, Q., Wang, L., Zhang, J., Song, Y., Jin, H., Zhang, K., Sun, H., Wang, H., Yang, B. 2013. Highly Photoluminescent Carbon Dots for Multicolor Patterning, Sensors, and Bioimaging. Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 52(14), 3953–3957.
